text: Chapter 6 introduces the formula-based construction of polytonal chords, establishing that altered chords are named mechanically — a C7b9b5 derives its formula (1, 3, b5, b7, b9) directly from the symbol without requiring lookup tables. The chapter presents formulas for the three basic chord families and their extensions as the canonical reference, and classifies diminished and augmented chords functionally with dominant chords, guiding their use in later progressions. A key load-bearing concept is the 9/11/13 ↔ 2/4/6 equivalence: extensions can be notated two ways (e.g., a 7/6 chord as 1, 3, 5, b7, 13), reflecting the dual treatment of compound and simple intervals. The master's characteristic move is systematic formula construction — students learn to parse chord symbols and generate voicings mechanically rather than memorizing isolated shapes.
